questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 2.2.2 Solutions of Ionic Solids in Water
questions.question: When an ionic solid dissolves in water, two competing energy factors determine solubility. What are these two factors?

options.D: Activation energy and heat of vaporization

questions.hint: One factor opposes dissolution, the other favors it.
questions.explanation: Lattice energy (energy to separate ions) opposes dissolution; hydration energy (energy released when water surrounds ions) favors dissolution. Option B applies to covalent bonds, not ionic dissolution.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.2 Solutions of Ionic Solids in Water
source_trace.content_anchor: lattice energy (the energy required to separate the ionic solute particles apart from a crystal lattice) and hydration energy

explanations_tiered.definition: Lattice energy is the energy required to separate ions in a crystal; hydration energy is the energy released when water surrounds ions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If hydration energy exceeds lattice energy, the compound is soluble; if lattice energy is larger, the compound is insoluble.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Lattice energy tries to keep the crystal together; hydration energy tries to pull ions apart. Whichever is stronger wins.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Lattice energy opposes dissolution (needs energy input). Hydration energy favors dissolution (releases energy).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The enthalpy of solution is ΔH_sol = ΔH_lattice + ΔH_hydration. For NaCl, ΔH_hydration ≈ -783 kJ/mol, ΔH_lattice ≈ +786 kJ/mol, so near zero net energy.
reasoning_steps: Identify the energy that holds the crystal together (lattice energy).
Identify the energy that solvates ions in water (hydration energy).
Compare their magnitudes to predict solubility.
misconceptions: Hydration energy is often incorrectly thought to be the same as heat of solution; it is only one component.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Solubility depends on lattice vs. hydration energy.

glossary_entry.definition: Energy required to separate one mole of an ionic solid into gaseous ions.

flashcard.front: What two energies determine solubility of ionic solids in water?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What two energies compete when an ionic solid dissolves?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy to separate ions in a crystal.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain solution formation for ionic solids.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 2.2.2 Solutions of Ionic Solids in Water
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Lattice energy opposes dissolution; hydration energy favors it.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 2.2.3 The Solubility Rules for Ionic Solids
questions.question: According to solubility rules, which of the following compounds is soluble in water?
questions.explanation: All common salts containing ammonium (NH₄⁺) are soluble. BaSO₄, AgCl, and CaCO₃ are all insoluble according to solubility rules (sulfate and carbonate exceptions, silver halide exception).

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.2.3 The Solubility Rules for Ionic Solids
source_trace.content_anchor: All common salts of the Group 1A elements and ammonium, NH4+ are soluble

explanations_tiered.definition: Solubility rules are guidelines predicting which ionic compounds dissolve in water.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ammonium nitrate contains NH₄⁺, which is always soluble. BaSO₄ is an insoluble sulfate; AgCl is an insoluble chloride; CaCO₃ is an insoluble carbonate.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Rule: All ammonium (NH₄⁺) salts dissolve in water. So NH₄NO₃ is soluble.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: BaSO₄: sulfate exception (Ba²⁺). AgCl: halide exception (Ag⁺). CaCO₃: carbonate (insoluble unless Group 1 or NH₄⁺).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solubility of NH₄NO₃ is high because both ions have low charge density and hydration energy exceeds lattice energy.
reasoning_steps: Recall that all ammonium and Group 1A salts are soluble.
Check each option for exceptions.
NH₄NO₃ contains NH₄⁺, so it is soluble.
misconceptions: All nitrates are soluble, but NH₄NO₃ is also an ammonium salt — doubly soluble.

glossary_entry.definition: Guidelines predicting solubility of ionic compounds in water.

flashcard.back: Yes. All ammonium salts are soluble.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which compound is soluble: BaSO₄, AgCl, NH₄NO₃, or CaCO₃?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: NH₄NO₃. Ammonium salts are always soluble.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Memorize the major solubility rule exceptions.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply solubility rules to predict solubility.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 2.2.3 The Solubility Rules for Ionic Solids
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. NH₄NO₃ contains ammonium, which is always soluble; the others are exceptions to solubility rules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which pair of liquids would be expected to be immiscible based on the 'like dissolves like' principle?
questions.hint: Polar and nonpolar liquids do not mix well.
questions.explanation: Water is polar; diethyl ether is nonpolar. 'Like dissolves like' predicts they are immiscible. Options A, B, and D are pairs of similar polarity (polar-polar or nonpolar-nonpolar) and are miscible.

source_trace.content_anchor: oil is nonpolar whereas water is polar → immiscible

explanations_tiered.definition: The 'like dissolves like' principle states that polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents and nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Water (polar) and diethyl ether (nonpolar) have different intermolecular forces, so they do not mix and form separate layers.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Polar water and nonpolar oil don't mix. Ether is like oil, so water and ether won't mix either.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Water forms hydrogen bonds; ether has only dispersion forces. No strong interactions exist between them, so they are immiscible.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Immiscibility occurs when the Gibbs free energy of mixing is positive. For water and ether, ΔH_mix is positive and large, preventing mixing.
reasoning_steps: Determine polarity of each liquid.
Apply 'like dissolves like': same polarity = miscible; different polarity = immiscible.
Identify pair with different polarities.
misconceptions: Diethyl ether has some polarity due to C-O bonds, but its net dipole moment is low, making it effectively nonpolar and immiscible with water.

glossary_entry.definition: Liquids that do not mix to form a single phase.

flashcard.front: Why are water and diethyl ether immiscible?
flashcard.back: Water is polar, ether is nonpolar; like dissolves like predicts no mixing.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Different polarities lead to weak intermolecular forces between them.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Liquids that do not dissolve in each other.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with water and oil, water and ether (in fume hood).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Water (polar) and diethyl ether (nonpolar) have different polarities, so they are immiscible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following will increase the rate of dissolution of a solid solute in a liquid solvent?
questions.hint: Stirring brings fresh solvent into contact with solute.
questions.explanation: Stirring increases the rate by moving dissolved solute away from the surface, allowing fresh solvent to contact undissolved solute. Option B decreases surface area, slowing dissolution.

source_trace.content_anchor: The increasing surface area of the solute will increase the rate of dissolution; stirring also increases rate

explanations_tiered.definition: Rate of dissolution is how quickly a solute dissolves, affected by surface area, temperature, and stirring.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Stirring increases contact between fresh solvent and solute surface, removing saturated solution from the interface.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Stirring your drink helps sugar dissolve faster because moving water brings fresh water to the sugar.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Agitation increases the rate by reducing the thickness of the boundary layer and maintaining a concentration gradient.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Noyes-Whitney equation shows dissolution rate ∝ D × A × (Cs - C)/h. Stirring increases the mass transfer coefficient (D/h), speeding dissolution.
reasoning_steps: Recall factors: surface area, temperature, stirring, particle size.
Identify which action directly increases contact with fresh solvent.
Stirring accomplishes this.
misconceptions: Stirring does not change solubility (maximum amount), only the rate to reach solubility.

glossary_entry.definition: Speed at which solute dissolves, influenced by stirring, temperature, and surface area.

flashcard.front: How does stirring affect dissolution rate?
flashcard.back: It increases the rate by bringing fresh solvent to the solute surface.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What action increases dissolution rate without changing solubility?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Stirring. It brings fresh solvent to the solute surface.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Speed of dissolving, increased by stirring and higher temperature.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with two beakers: one stirred, one unstirred.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe factors affecting rate of dissolution.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Stirring increases contact between solvent and solute, speeding dissolution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 2.5.1 Preparation of Stock Solutions from Pure Compound
questions.sub_subsection: A) preparation of liquid solution using a solid solute
questions.structural_anchor: A) preparation of liquid solution using a solid solute
questions.question: When preparing a solution by dissolving a solid solute, why should the solid be dissolved in less than the final volume before transferring to a volumetric flask?

options.B: To ensure complete dissolution and accurate final volume
options.C: To speed up the weighing process

questions.hint: Allows mixing before final dilution to the mark.
questions.explanation: Dissolving in a smaller volume first ensures all solute is fully dissolved; then dilution to the mark gives accurate concentration. Option A is not the primary reason.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 2.5.1 Preparation of Stock Solutions from Pure Compound
source_trace.structural_element: A) preparation of liquid solution using a solid solute
source_trace.content_anchor: The amount of solvent added at this stage should not exceed half the final volume

explanations_tiered.definition: Volumetric flasks are designed for precise final volume after all solute is fully dissolved.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Pre-dissolving in partial solvent ensures no undissolved particles remain, then diluting to the mark yields exact concentration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You need to make sure all the sugar is dissolved before adding water to the fill line, or the final amount will be wrong.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Undissolved solute would increase the final volume when diluted to the mark, causing concentration error.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Partial dissolution avoids volume contraction/expansion anomalies when solids dissolve; final dilution in the calibrated flask ensures accuracy.
reasoning_steps: Add less than half the final solvent volume.
Stir to dissolve completely.
Transfer to volumetric flask.
Dilute to calibration mark.
misconceptions: Adding solid directly to a full volumetric flask may prevent complete mixing and accurate volume.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Dissolve in small volume first, then dilute to mark.

glossary_entry.definition: Calibrated flask for precise solution preparation.

flashcard.front: Why dissolve solid in less than final volume before diluting?
flashcard.back: Ensures complete dissolution and accurate final concentration.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the correct procedure for dissolving solid in a volumetric flask?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Dissolve in less than final volume first, then dilute to mark.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Flask calibrated to contain precise volume.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate proper use of volumetric flask.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 2.5.1 Preparation of Stock Solutions from Pure Compound
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Pre-dissolving ensures all solute is in solution before final dilution to the mark.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which term describes a solution that contains the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a given temperature?
questions.hint: This solution is at equilibrium with undissolved solute.
questions.explanation: A saturated solution contains the maximum solute concentration at that temperature, in equilibrium with solid solute. Option C is unstable and contains more than the maximum.

explanations_tiered.definition: A saturated solution contains the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve at a given temperature.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: At saturation, dissolution rate equals recrystallization rate. Adding more solute leaves it undissolved.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like a sponge that has soaked up all the water it can — no more will go in.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Saturated solutions are at dynamic equilibrium with excess solid present, if any.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The solubility limit at a given temperature defines saturation. Any additional solute will not dissolve.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of saturated solution from key terms.
Contrast with unsaturated (can dissolve more) and supersaturated (unstable).
misconceptions: Saturated does not mean 'thick' or 'concentrated' in a general sense; it means at solubility limit.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Saturated = maximum solute at given T.

glossary_entry.definition: Solution containing the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a specific temperature.

flashcard.back: A solution that contains the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a given temperature.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A solution containing the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a given temperature.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Saturated = max solute at given T.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define saturated, unsaturated, and supersaturated solutions.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Saturated solution is at maximum solute concentration for a given temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to the unit summary, which type of mixture exhibits the Tyndall effect and has particle sizes between 1 nm and 100 nm?
questions.hint: These particles are larger than solutions but smaller than suspensions.
questions.explanation: Colloids have particle sizes 1–100 nm and scatter light (Tyndall effect). Solutions have smaller particles, suspensions have larger particles that settle.

source_trace.content_anchor: A colloid is a heterogeneous mixture in which insoluble particles of one or more substances are suspended uniformly. Scattering of a beam of light by colloidal particles is called Tyndall effect.

explanations_tiered.definition: A colloid is a mixture with particle sizes 1–100 nm that exhibits the Tyndall effect.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Tyndall effect distinguishes colloids from true solutions; particle size determines mixture type.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Colloids are in between — bigger than solution particles but smaller than suspension particles.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Colloidal particles scatter light (Tyndall effect) because their size is comparable to light wavelength.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Tyndall effect results from Mie scattering; colloid particle diameter is 1–100 nm, suspensions larger, solutions smaller.
reasoning_steps: Recall particle size ranges: solution <1 nm, colloid 1–100 nm, suspension >100 nm.
Light scattering occurs for particles in colloid range.
misconceptions: Suspensions also scatter light initially, but particles settle; colloids remain suspended and continuously scatter.

glossary_entry.definition: Heterogeneous mixture with particle sizes 1–100 nm that scatters light.

flashcard.front: What mixture type has particle sizes 1–100 nm and shows Tyndall effect?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What mixture type has particles 1–100 nm and Tyndall effect?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Mixture with intermediate particle size (1–100 nm).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use laser pointer through milk dilution vs. salt water.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish among solutions, colloids, and suspensions.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Colloids have particle sizes 1–100 nm and exhibit Tyndall effect.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Soda water is an example of which type of solution according to the Review Exercise?
questions.hint: Soda water contains dissolved carbon dioxide gas in water.
questions.explanation: Soda water is a solution of carbon dioxide gas (solute) dissolved in liquid water (solvent), making it a liquid-gas solution. Option A would be two liquids mixed, like ethanol and water.

source_trace.content_anchor: Question 1: Soda water is an example of: b. liquid-gas solution

explanations_tiered.definition: A liquid-gas solution has a gas solute dissolved in a liquid solvent.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Soda water is carbonated: CO₂ gas is the solute, water is the solvent.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Bubbles in soda are carbon dioxide gas dissolved in the liquid water.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Carbonated beverages are pressurized with CO₂ gas, which dissolves in water, forming a liquid-gas solution.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Henry's law describes the solubility of CO₂ in water; when pressure is released, CO₂ comes out of solution, forming bubbles.
reasoning_steps: Identify solute and solvent in soda water.
Solute = CO₂ (gas), solvent = water (liquid).
Classify as liquid-gas solution.
misconceptions: The bubbles themselves are not the solution; the dissolved CO₂ before bubbling is.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Soda water = liquid-gas solution (CO₂ in H₂O).

glossary_entry.definition: A solution with a gas solute dissolved in a liquid solvent.

flashcard.front: What type of solution is soda water?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Is soda water liquid-gas or liquid-liquid?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Liquid-gas. CO₂ gas dissolved in water.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to Henry's law and pressure.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Soda water contains CO₂ gas dissolved in liquid water, making it a liquid-gas solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following type of matter can exhibit the Tyndall effect?
questions.hint: Tyndall effect occurs in colloids, including aerosols.
questions.explanation: Aerosols (e.g., fog, smoke) are colloids and scatter light. Soda water is a true solution, brass is a solid solution, pure water is a pure substance — none show Tyndall effect.

source_trace.content_anchor: Question 2: Which of the following type of matter can exhibit Tyndall effect? a. aerosol

explanations_tiered.definition: The Tyndall effect is light scattering by colloidal particles, which occurs in aerosols (liquid/gas colloids).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Aerosols are colloids (e.g., fog, smoke) with particle sizes 1–100 nm that scatter light. Solutions and pure substances do not scatter light.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Aerosols like fog or smoke scatter light; you can see the beam. Soda water and clean water do not.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: True solutions have particles too small to scatter visible light. Brass is a solid solution with atoms, not colloid-size domains.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Tyndall effect requires a refractive index difference between dispersed and continuous phases, present in aerosols but not in homogeneous solutions.
reasoning_steps: Recall that Tyndall effect = light scattering by colloids.
Identify which options are colloids.
Aerosols are gas-liquid or gas-solid colloids.
misconceptions: Any cloudy liquid shows Tyndall effect, but clear solutions do not.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Tyndall effect: colloids only → e.g., aerosols.

glossary_entry.definition: Colloidal dispersion of liquid or solid particles in a gas.

flashcard.front: Which mixture type shows Tyndall effect?
flashcard.back: Colloids, including aerosols like fog and smoke.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of matter exhibits Tyndall effect?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify mixtures that show Tyndall effect.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Aerosols are colloids and exhibit Tyndall effect; soda water, brass, and pure water do not.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: How many grams of NaOH are required to prepare 100 mL of 1 M NaOH solution?
questions.hint: Moles = M × V(L); mass = moles × molar mass.
questions.explanation: Moles NaOH = 1.0 M × 0.100 L = 0.100 mol. Mass = 0.100 mol × 40.0 g/mol = 4.0 g. Option B is for 1 L, not 100 mL.

source_trace.content_anchor: Question 9: How many grams of NaOH is required to prepare 100 mL of 1 M NaOH solution? a. 4

explanations_tiered.definition: Molarity is moles per liter; to find mass, multiply molarity by volume (L) by molar mass.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 1 M means 1 mole per liter. For 0.1 liter, you need 0.1 mole. 0.1 mole of NaOH is 4 grams.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Molar mass NaOH = 40.00 g/mol. Mass needed = (1.00 mol/L)(0.100 L)(40.00 g/mol) = 4.00 g.
worked_solution: Step 2: Moles NaOH = 1.0 M × 0.100 L = 0.100 mol.
reasoning_steps: Convert volume to liters.
Calculate moles from molarity and liters.
Multiply moles by molar mass to get grams.
misconceptions: Using 100 mL instead of 0.100 L in calculation leads to 100× error.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: For 1 M in 100 mL: mass = 1 × 0.1 × 40 = 4 g.

glossary_entry.definition: Moles of solute per liter of solution.

flashcard.front: What mass NaOH for 100 mL of 1 M solution?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What mass NaOH for 100 mL of 1 M?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Practice with different volumes and molarities.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate mass required for molar solution.
